steps.title: Use the experiment result
steps.content: The experiment compares two groups: one group touched a mug and a scarf, and the other group was not allowed to touch the items. The people who touched the products were willing to pay much more for them. The conclusion must connect physical touch with higher perceived value.

steps.title: Avoid unsupported leaps
steps.content: Choice A makes a broad claim about monthly spending, but the experiment never measures how much online or in-person shoppers spend each month. Choice C shifts to what retailers should charge, which is also outside the evidence. Choice D concerns online-only products, but the result favors touch, not increased pricing power for items customers cannot touch.

steps.title: Choose the direct inference
steps.content: Choice B stays within the evidence. In-person shopping gives customers the chance to touch products, while online shopping cannot replicate that experience. Since touch made participants willing to pay more, in-person shopping may make products seem more valuable than they seem online. The answer is B.
